perturbation.original_text: 1.3. An Agent of the Investor is an authorized person of the investor who has access to the Proprietary Information. The Proprietary Information is any information of the Company marked as «Commercial Secret» or «Conﬁdential» with actual or potential commercial value by virtue of the fact third-parties are unfamiliar with it, submitted to the Investor by the Company in written form by documents transmission, including the electronic transmission of documents secured from unauthorized access to the transmitted information, the information related to business or ﬁnance plans and strategies including, without limitation information about markets, ﬁnancial documents, ﬁnancial statements and accounting (except for cases deﬁned by law), contractual relationships, pricing and marketing of goods (works and services), technical information, commercial secrets, know-how, research, production schedules, concepts, intellectual activity property (which includes discoveries, inventions, rationalization proposals, utility models, constructions, industrial models that are non-proprietary for some reasons, computer software, databases, logotypes scratches that are non-registered for some reasons).
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3. An Agent of the Investor is an authorized person of the investor who has access to the Proprietary Information. The Proprietary Information is any information of the Company, whether marked as «Commercial Secret» or «Conﬁdential» or not, regardless of its commercial value or the familiarity of third parties with it, submitted to the Investor by the Company in any form, including electronic transmission, regardless of security measures for the transmitted information, related to business or ﬁnance plans and strategies including information about markets, ﬁnancial documents, ﬁnancial statements and accounting, contractual relationships, pricing and marketing of goods, technical information, commercial secrets, know-how, research, production schedules, concepts, intellectual property (including discoveries, inventions, rationalization proposals, utility models, constructions, industrial models, computer software, databases, and logotypes).
perturbation.explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of Proprietary Information to include any information regardless of its marking, commercial value, or security measures. This conflicts with the standard definition requiring reasonable measures to maintain secrecy for information to be considered a trade secret.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Trade Secrets Law - specifically, the requirements for information to qualify as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 40(1) defines confidential information, which could be relevant in trade secret cases. The modified text's disregard for whether the information is marked confidential or its commercial value directly contradicts the requirements for establishing a trade secret. For information to be protected as a trade secret, there needs to be an element of confidentiality and commercial value, and the company must take steps to maintain that confidentiality. This modification weakens the NDA by trying to protect information that may not legally qualify as a trade secret under Singapore law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: tronique. « Art. R. 331-37. − Les opérateurs de communications électroniques mentionnés à l’article L. 34-1 du code des postes et des communications électroniques et les prestataires mentionnés aux 1 et 2 du I de l’article 6 de . . 27 juillet 2010 JOURNAL OFFICIEL DE LA RÉPUBLIQUE FRANÇAISE Texte 44 sur 126 la loi no 2004-575 du 21 juin 2004 pour la confiance dans l’économie numérique sont tenus de communiquer les données à caractère personnel et les informations mentionnées au 2o de l’annexe du décret no 2010-236 du 5 mars 2010 dans un délai de huit jours suivant la transmission par la commission de protection des droits des données techniques nécessaires à l’identification de l’abonné dont l’accès à des services de communication au public en ligne a été utilisé à des fins de reproduction, de représentation, de mise à disposition ou de communication au public d’œuvres ou d’objets protégés sans l’autorisation des titulaires des droits prévus aux livres Ier et II lorsqu’elle est requise. « Ces opérateurs et prestataires sont également tenus de fournir les documents et les copies des documents mentionnés aux troisième et quatrième alinéas de l’article L. 331-21 dans un délai de quinze jours suivant la demande qui leur en est faite par la commission de protection des droits. « Art. R. 331-38. − Est puni de l’amende prévue pour les contraventions de cinquième classe le fait de contrevenir aux dispositions de l’article R. 331-37. « La récidive des contraventions prévues au présent article est réprimée conformément aux articles 132-11 et 132-15 du code pénal. « Art. R. 331-39. − Toute demande ou toute observation adressée à la commission de protection des droits par le destinataire d’une recommandation visée au premier ou au deuxième alinéa de l’article L. 331-25 n’est instruite que si elle comporte le numéro de dossier figurant dans cette recommandation. « Il est accusé réception de la demande ou de l’observation par la commission de protection des droits. « Art. R. 331-40. − Lorsque, dans le délai d’un an suivant la présentation de la recommandation mentionnée au premier alinéa de l’article L. 335-7-1, la commission de protection des droits est saisie de nouveaux faits susceptibles de constituer une négligence caractérisée définie à l’article R. 335-5, elle informe l’abonné, par lettre remise contre signature, que ces faits sont susceptibles de poursuite. Cette lettre invite l’intéressé à présenter ses observations dans un délai de quinze jours. Elle précise qu’il peut, dans le même délai, solliciter une audition en application de l’article L. 331-21-1 et qu’il a droit de se faire assister par un conseil. Elle l’invite également à préciser ses charges de famille et ses ressources. « La commission peut de sa propre initiative convoquer l’intéressé aux fins d’audition. La lettre de convocation précise qu’il a droit de se faire assister par un conseil. « Art. R. 331-41. − Il est dressé procès-verbal de l’audition de l’intéressé par un membre de la commission de protection des droits ou par un agent habilité et assermenté en application de l’article R. 331-16. « Le procès-verbal est signé par l’intéressé et par son conseil, par la personne procédant à l’audition ainsi que par celle qui l’a rédigé. Si la personne entendue ou son conseil ne veut pas signer le procès-verbal, mention en est portée sur celui-ci. « Une copie du procès-verbal est remise à l’intéressé. « Art. R. 331-42. − La commission de protection des droits constate par une délibération prise à la majorité d’au moins deux voix que les faits sont susceptibles de constituer l’infraction prévue à l’article R. 335-5 ou les infractions prévues aux articles L. 335-2, L. 335-3 et L. 335-4. « Toutefois, lorsque seuls deux membres de la commission sont présents et en cas de partage des voix, l’examen de la procédure est renvoyé à la première séance plénière de la commission. « Art. R. 331-43. − La délibération de la commission constatant que les faits sont susceptibles de constituer
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of Proprietary Information, disregarding whether the information is marked confidential, its commercial value, or if security measures are in place. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that this contradicts the requirement for information to have an element of confidentiality and commercial value to be protected as a trade secret. While the provided law snippets are not directly relevant, the core principle that trade secrets require an effort to maintain secrecy is a widely accepted legal standard. The change significantly weakens the protection of trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction by attempting to protect information that might not legally qualify as such.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1. The Investor is authorized to grant access to the proprietary information only to those parties who require access to the proprietary information in realizing the investors’ rights under the Offer in order to achieve the provision of the Proprietary Information, are obliged not to disclose the Proprietary Information and informed about the fact of the conclusion of the Agreement. The Investor is obliged to provide the list of Investor’s Agents who have received access to the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1. The Investor may grant access to the proprietary information to any party, regardless of their need to know or their agreement to maintain confidentiality. The Investor is not obligated to provide a list of parties who have received access to the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Investor to share proprietary information with anyone, undermining the entire purpose of the non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_citation: Singapore Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore contract law includes an implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. This modification, allowing unrestricted disclosure, effectively negates the core purpose of the NDA and violates the implied duty of good faith, making the contract potentially unenforceable as it permits actions that are contrary to the reasonable expectations of the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of good faith dealing, which is implied in Singapore contract law. By allowing the Investor to share proprietary information with anyone, regardless of confidentiality, the modification negates the purpose of the NDA. The law snippet states that 'good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent .' The modification permits actions contrary to fair dealing, undermining the expectation that the proprietary information will be protected. Furthermore, the investor is no longer obligated to provide a list of parties, which directly impedes accountability and faithful performance of their duties under the NDA.

perturbation.original_text: 3.4. The Investor is obliged to keep, withhold, and hold any conﬁdential information, take measures, which are greater than or equal to measures which the Investor takes to protect his or her own conﬁdential information, to secure the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.4. The Investor is required to take only minimal precautions, if any, to protect the Proprietary Information. The Investor's efforts to protect the Company's Proprietary Information do not need to be consistent with the measures taken to protect the Investor's own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: This reduces the standard of care required to protect the information, creating a legal contradiction with industry best practices and potentially invalidating the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Trade Secrets Law - reasonable steps to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: To claim trade secret protection in Singapore, the owner must take reasonable steps to maintain secrecy. By stating that minimal precautions are sufficient, this clause contradicts the established legal principle that 'reasonable steps' are necessary, thereby jeopardizing the enforceability of the NDA regarding trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the standard of care required to protect proprietary information. The original text mandates measures 'greater than or equal to' the investor's own confidential information protection, while the changed text requires 'only minimal precautions, if any.' The Law Explanation states that to claim trade secret protection, 'reasonable steps to maintain secrecy' are necessary. By allowing minimal precautions, the changed text contradicts the principle of 'reasonable steps,' which jeopardizes the enforceability of the NDA.

perturbation.original_text: 3.6. The Investor shall be entitled to make copies, abstracts, memoranda, or other documents (including documents made by mechanical or electronic means) related to the conﬁdential information, which shall also be marked as «Commercial Secret» and/or «Conﬁdential» only to the extents that are necessary for the conﬁdential information achievement. The Investor shall provide the true recording of all copies and places where the duplicates copies of the conﬁdential information are kept, and access limitation to the speciﬁed copies, abstracts, memoranda, and other documents to third-parties except for his or her authorized Agents, according to the section 3.1. of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6. The Investor may make unlimited copies of the Proprietary Information without marking them as «Commercial Secret» or «Conﬁdential». The Investor is not required to keep records of these copies or limit access to them.
perturbation.explanation: Removing restrictions on copying and marking the information weakens the protection of the information, contradicting standard practices for trade secret protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Trade Secrets Law - requirements for maintaining secrecy
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly undermines efforts to maintain the secrecy of the information. Failing to mark copies as confidential and allowing unlimited, unrecorded copying makes it difficult to prove that the company took reasonable steps to protect its trade secrets, thus diminishing its legal standing in a trade secret misappropriation claim under Singapore law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirements to mark copies of confidential information as such and to keep records of those copies. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this weakens the protection of the information and contradicts standard practices for trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that failing to mark copies and allowing unlimited copying makes it difficult to prove that the company took reasonable steps to protect its trade secrets. Although the provided snippets do not explicitly address this point, the reasoning provided indicates a contradiction based on the modification's impact on trade secret protection. The changed text significantly reduces the measures taken to protect confidential information, which could undermine a company's ability to legally defend its trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1. The party who fails to encompass its obligations under the Agreement is obliged to recoup the other party for damages caused by disclosure or misuse of the conﬁdential information. The damages shall be recouped according to Singapore legislation.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1. The party who fails to fulfill its obligations under the Agreement is only liable for nominal damages, not exceeding $100 Singapore dollars, regardless of the actual damages caused by the disclosure or misuse of the confidential information. Damages are capped regardless of Singapore legislation.
perturbation.explanation: This severely limits the potential damages, making enforcement of the NDA practically useless.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - principles of damages for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_citation: Singapore Contract Law - Principles of Damages
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Singapore contract law, damages for breach are intended to compensate the injured party for their losses. Limiting damages to a nominal amount, such as $100, regardless of the actual harm suffered, contradicts the compensatory principle of damages and could render the clause, and potentially the agreement, unenforceable if the court finds it unconscionable or against public policy.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of compensatory damages in contract law. The original text states damages will be recouped according to Singapore legislation, implying full compensation for damages. The changed text limits damages to a nominal $100, regardless of actual harm, undermining the compensatory principle. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that such a limitation 'contradicts the compensatory principle of damages'. The scraped snippets, while not directly on point, do not contradict this interpretation.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1. The Agreement shall enter into force as soon as it has been accepted by the Investor and also shall be effective during 5 (ﬁve) years from the termination of the effective period of the Offer, so that the Investor shall fulﬁll its obligations of conﬁdentiality for 5 (ﬁve) years following the expiration of the Agreement or Offer.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1. The Agreement shall be effective only for a period of 1 (one) day from the date of acceptance, after which all confidentiality obligations are immediately terminated.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the duration of the agreement to one day nullifies the purpose of the NDA, as it provides almost no protection for confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Reasonableness and Intent of Parties
perturbation.law_citation: Singapore Contract Law - Intent of Parties
perturbation.law_explanation: A court in Singapore would look to the intent of the parties when interpreting a contract. An NDA that is only effective for one day contradicts the presumed intent of the parties to protect confidential information for a reasonable period. Such a short duration calls into question the entire purpose of the agreement and could be seen as commercially unreasonable, potentially leading a court to disregard or reform the clause.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a confidentiality period of 5 years following the termination of the offer, which aligns with the typical intent of an NDA to protect sensitive information for a reasonable duration. The changed text reduces this period to just one day, effectively negating the protection. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that a short duration 'calls into question the entire purpose of the agreement and could be seen as commercially unreasonable'. While the scraped law snippets don't directly address NDA durations, the principle of intent and commercial reasonableness, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, suggests that this change creates a strong contradiction, as it renders the NDA practically useless.
